title: AI-Native Document Protocol (v1.0)

nodes.summary: Defines the AI-Native Document Protocol as a dual-substrate architecture for multi-plane cognition, binding human meaning and AI geometry into a single coherent system.
Introduces the motivation, problem, and solution for AI-native documents in multi-plane cognition.
Explains why linear documents fail in multi-plane cognition, why humans cannot hold multi-layer meaning, why AI collapses without invariants, and why DSLO requires a new communication substrate.
Defines ontology mismatch, drift across planes, loss of geometry, human-to-human relational collapse, and AI-to-human instability as core failure modes.
Summarizes the dual-substrate architecture, AI-mediated reading, AI-stabilized writing, geometry-anchored meaning, and DPS as the executable layer.
Describes the dual-substrate architecture: Writing Substrate and Reading Substrate, and why both are required.
Defines node encoding, invariant anchoring, constraint definition, semantic compression, and geometry-first authoring as the core of the Writing Substrate.
Defines the AI-Native Reading Protocol, including expansion vectors, cross-plane translation, drift correction, and structural verification.
Explains why two substrates are required due to human cognitive limits, AI ontology fluidity, multi-plane coherence needs, and geometry preservation.
Specifies the AI-Native Writing Protocol, including roles of author and AI, the dual-substrate writing loop, and authoring constraints.
Defines the author’s role: provide meaning, nodes, vectors, and constraints; avoid narrative drift and legacy ontology.
Defines the AI’s role: condense, stabilize, enforce invariants, align geometry, and produce substrate-safe form.
Describes the loop: Human -> Meaning, AI -> Geometry, Human -> Verification, AI -> Finalization.
Lists constraints: no implicit assumptions, no linear dependencies, no uncontrolled metaphors, no narrative-first structure.
Defines the AI-Native Reading Protocol, including reader and AI roles, the reading loop, and multi-plane navigation.
Defines the reader’s role: explore rather than consume, query nodes, trigger expansions, and request cross-plane mapping.
Defines the AI’s role: expand, explain, translate, correct drift, and maintain invariants during reading.
Describes the loop: Reader -> Explore, AI -> Expand, AI -> Explain, AI -> Correct, Reader -> Stabilize.
Defines navigation across surface, relational, geometric, and substrate planes.
Explains why geometry is the meaning substrate and how documents become geometric objects with AI-preserved behavior.
Explains that geometry provides stability, invariance, drift resistance, and multi-plane compatibility.
Treats the document as a geometric object: nodes as coordinates, vectors as relations, boundaries as constraints, morphisms as transitions.
Defines geometry-preserving AI behavior: topological legality, invariant enforcement, boundary preservation, and correction operators.
Describes integration with the DSLO Geometric Field Substrate and DSLO Product Specification (DPS), and the document-to-DPS pipeline.
Defines the DSLO Geometric Field Substrate: latent manifold M, invariant potential operator, triadic invariants, and virtual Hilbert space.
Describes the DSLO Product Specification (DPS): executable geometry, deterministic kernels, boundary conditions, correction rules, and portable execution graph.
Defines the pipeline: Meaning -> Geometry -> Execution, mapping AI-native documents into DPS.
Introduces the Fixed-Point Layer expressed in 5-year-old language as a universal anchor for drift-free meaning.
Defines the purpose of the fixed point: universal anchor, plane-neutral meaning, drift-free entry point.
Lists canonical expressions such as 'This is the rule that keeps the rules from drifting.' and 'This is the boundary that keeps the meaning from leaking.'
Explains how AI uses the fixed point for ontology alignment, reader grounding, and drift correction.
Describes the full communication loop across author, AI, substrate, DPS, and production.
Defines the author-to-AI path: Meaning -> Geometry.
Defines the reader-to-AI path: Exploration -> Expansion.
Defines the AI-to-substrate path: Geometry -> Invariants.
Defines the substrate-to-DPS path: Invariants -> Execution.
Defines the DPS-to-production path: Deterministic output.
Explains why the AI-Native Document Protocol is necessary given human cognitive limits, AI properties, DSLO requirements, and failure modes without the protocol.
Describes human limits: single-plane reasoning, limited working memory, ontology rigidity.
Describes AI properties: multi-plane reasoning, ontology fluidity, high-dimensional geometry.
Defines DSLO requirements: invariant preservation, geometry stability, multi-plane coherence.
Lists failure modes without the protocol: drift, collapse, misinterpretation, loss of invariants.
Summarizes the protocol, its implications, and future work.
Summarizes dual-substrate communication, AI-mediated cognition, geometry-anchored meaning, and deterministic execution.
Describes implications: post-linear scientific communication, deterministic AI reasoning, executable knowledge artifacts, and multi-plane human-AI collaboration.
Outlines future work: multi-agent substrate protocols, on-device substrate runtimes, and real-time geometric cognition.

invariants: Meaning is unfolded interactively, not consumed linearly.
Nodes are semantic anchors, not prose.
Geometry defines the legal option space for interpretation.
